Motivation

A short paragraph framing the empirical question — e.g. “central banks have long struggled to explain [X]; this note replicates [paper/report] and checks whether the result holds with more recent Brazilian data.”

title: Exercise template — TODO: real title
text:
Data

For now, a synthetic quarterly series stands in for the real data pull:


Show code
set.seed(42)
n <- 80
series <- arima.sim(model = list(ar = 0.6), n = n) |> as.numeric()
dates <- seq.Date(from = as.Date("2006-01-01"), by = "quarter", length.out = n)
df <- data.frame(date = dates, y = series)
tail(df)


         date       y
75 2024-07-01 -0.3268
76 2024-10-01  0.7373
77 2025-01-01  1.2641
78 2025-04-01  2.1506
79 2025-07-01  0.8142
80 2025-10-01  1.1389

title: Exercise template — TODO: real title
text:
Method

A simple AR(1) forecasting model:
\[
y_t = \phi \, y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)
\]

title: Exercise template — TODO: real title
text:
Code


Show code
fit <- arima(df$y, order = c(1, 0, 0))
fc <- predict(fit, n.ahead = 4)

fc_df <- data.frame(
  date = seq.Date(from = max(df$date), by = "quarter", length.out = 5)[-1],
  y = as.numeric(fc$pred),
  lower = as.numeric(fc$pred - 1.96 * fc$se),
  upper = as.numeric(fc$pred + 1.96 * fc$se)
)

fit



Call:
arima(x = df$y, order = c(1, 0, 0))

Coefficients:
        ar1  intercept
      0.596     -0.043
s.e.  0.089      0.275

sigma^2 estimated as 1.02:  log likelihood = -114.5,  aic = 235

title: Exercise template — TODO: real title
text:
Figure


Show code
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(df, aes(date, y)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0f766e") +
  geom_ribbon(data = fc_df, aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = 0.15, fill = "#14b8a6") +
  geom_line(data = fc_df, color = "#14b8a6", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)








Figure 1: TODO: real caption. Synthetic AR(1) series with a 4-quarter forecast.

title: About
text:
Raphael Ludwig
Quantitative economist with five years at Vista Capital, where I helped build a macro research desk from the ground up — covering Brazil and Europe, developing forecasting and nowcasting models, and applying AI to research workflows. MSc in Economics from PUC-Rio.
I’m currently looking for new opportunities in quantitative/macro research roles. This site collects exercises that replicate and extend public empirical work — central bank studies, academic papers, and sell-side research — as a demonstration of method.
raphaludwig@gmail.com · LinkedIn  · CV (PDF)

title: About
text:
Experience
Economist — Vista Capital · Dec 2020 – May 2026
Structuring the desk, data infrastructure and operational routines

Structured, alongside chief economist Alexandre Maia, the Macro Research area from its inception — defining processes, analytical routines, data infrastructure, and research products used daily by the fund.
Built a proprietary macroeconomic data platform running on Docker on a Linux VM (later migrated to Prefect), with autonomous monitoring of updates, alerts, and automatic reports.
Maintained 250+ internal routines for collecting, validating, and disseminating global data, focused on robustness, traceability, and fast reaction to releases.

Modeling, quantitative research and applied AI

Developed and coordinated the architecture of macro projection models, including a proprietary model-selection algorithm and an automated short/medium-term forecasting system.
Replicated and adapted empirical exercises from central banks, academic papers, policy blogs, and sell-side research reports.
Built internal AI-based tools (using Claude Code), including dashboard/workflow automation, routine and algorithm optimization, and data infrastructure.

Macro coverage, policy, and insight generation

Led Brazil coverage — combining data monitoring, quantitative modeling, macro-political scenario analysis, trips to Brasília, meetings with policy actors, and internal scenario discussions.
Led Europe coverage — focused on the Euro Area and the UK, extending to Eastern Europe when relevant, integrating data, macro-political scenario, fiscal risk, inflation, activity, elections, and central banks.
Led thematic coverage on topics with significant macro/market impact, including Covid-19 and supply-chain disruption.
Produced and presented weekly briefings on relevant macro topics; participated daily in the morning news call, responsible for the initial Brazil and Europe read.

Training, technical leadership and talent development

Trained multiple intern cohorts in programming and macro topics, and collaborated on their applied research, including thesis supervision.
Led internal trainings on economics, programming, automation, and applied AI — including a corporate crash course on AI and Claude Code usage.

title: About
text:
Education
MSc in Economics — PUC-Rio · Jan 2019 – Jul 2021 Dissertation: “On the Missing Disinflation Puzzle: A Data-Driven Approach”, advised by Prof. Eduardo Zilberman and Prof. Marcelo Medeiros.
BSc in Economics — PUC-Rio · Jan 2013 – Jul 2017 Participated in the PET and PIBIC research programs with Prof. Pedro Souza (Queen Mary University of London), focused on forecasting models and machine-learning applications. Teaching assistant for Programming Techniques for Economics II, applied to microeconomics, forecasting, and time series. Undergraduate thesis: “Nowcasting Unemployment with Google Trends: Evidence from the Brazilian Labor Market”, advised by Prof. Pedro Souza.

title: About
text:
Skills
Quantitative macro research — forecasting, nowcasting, short/medium-term models, applied econometrics, machine learning, macro-political scenario analysis.
Programming & data — R, Python, MATLAB/Octave, Dynare, Git.
Infrastructure & automation — Docker, Linux/VMs, Prefect, Airflow, automated pipelines, Slack integrations, Shiny, Streamlit, JavaScript.
Applied research & AI — replication of empirical exercises from central banks, academic papers, and sell-side research; using Claude Code for automation, refactoring, and routine optimization.

title: About
text:
Beyond economics
Educated at Escola Alemã Corcovado (German School) through the German track, completing the Abitur among the top three of the year. Federated volleyball player through adolescence, team captain across several seasons. Ongoing interest in music, literature, film, photography, and philosophy.

title: Exercises
text:
A growing collection of quantitative economics exercises — replications and extensions of public empirical work from central banks, academic papers, and sell-side research, built on public data.
